message.content: we just chivvied a pace into BS regarding rethinking how payor reauthorization should happen.  can you locate it?  I have some thoughts I want to add to it if we can find it

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Chivvy enrolls first, so the new pace should be at the top — `₢BSAAF`. Let me look at it.

## Character

Interactive design conversation requiring operator judgment — do NOT
implement. A strategic reorientation that should reshape this heat's tactical
paces before they are cut; its product is an agreed direction, not code.

## Trigger

Surfaced live while first-running the ₣BM skirmish suite. The project's active
Payor moved from a **consumer** Google account (`bhyslop@gmail.com`, no org) to
an **org-affiliated** Workspace identity (`bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`, org
`247899326218`). The operator's read: this is a major use-style change, not a
one-off config tweak, and the project must be reoriented to treat
org-affiliated accounts as a first-class mode.

## What we proved this session (the durable reminder)

Org-affiliated identities inherit secure-by-default posture the prior consumer
account never had — two distinct behaviors, two distinct layers:

- **Reauth cadence.** The org's session/reauth policy refuses the payor OAuth
  refresh token after a short window (a ~22h-old token returned `invalid_rapt`;
  `rbw-gPI` re-auth cleared it). `rbw-gPR` is stale here — its text assumes the
  consumer "6-month idle expiry" model, which no longer describes reality.
- **Key-creation block.** `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` is `enforced`
  by default on the standing depot, so governor/retriever/director SA-key
  creation will be refused — the wall the mantle would hit next.
- The payor **lacks** `orgpolicy.policy.set` but **holds**
  `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (so it could self-grant
  policyAdmin). The standing depot is ACTIVE under the org; no levy needed.

## Goal

Decide, honestly and with the operator, how org-affiliated accounts become a
supported use style: where reauth cadence and the key-creation override are
surfaced, owned, and documented across establish / levy / onboarding — and
whether the tactical org-policy paces already slated in this heat are the right
shape or need recasting in light of the above. Output: an agreed design
direction written back into the paddock, with the downstream paces reshaped to
match.

## Constraint

Produces understanding and a reshaped plan, not code. Implementation belongs to
the downstream paces, governed by this pace's conclusions.

content.text:
Found it: **₢BSAAF** in heat ₣BS — the most recently chivvied pace (sits first in the heat).

**Silks/purpose:** an interactive design conversation about reorienting the project to treat **org-affiliated Google accounts as a first-class mode**, triggered live during the ₣BM skirmish run when the active Payor moved from `bhyslop@gmail.com` (consumer, no org) to `bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` (Workspace, org `247899326218`).

The docket already captures three durable findings from this session:
- **Reauth cadence** — org session policy refused the ~22h-old payor refresh token (`invalid_rapt`); `rbw-gPR`'s "6-month idle expiry" text is now stale.
- **Key-creation block** — `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` enforced by default, will refuse governor/retriever/director SA-key creation.
- **Policy permissions** — payor lacks `orgpolicy.policy.set` but holds `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (could self-grant policyAdmin); standing depot ACTIVE, no levy needed.

Its character is explicitly *design conversation, no implementation* — output is an agreed direction written back to the paddock with downstream paces reshaped.

What thoughts do you want to add? I can fold them into the docket via a reslate (`jjx_redocket`) — tell me whether they're additional findings, a refinement of the goal, or new constraints, and I'll integrate them while preserving the existing structure.

message.content: Good.  Now, seek out a memo that considers revising the current 'authentication files' to use formal google identities for our service account roles.  Can you find that one?

content.text: I'll search the Memos directory for one about authentication files and formal Google identities for service account roles.

lastPrompt: Good.  Now, seek out a memo that considers revising the current 'authentication files' to use formal google identities for our service account roles.  Can you find that one?

input.prompt:
Search the directory /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/ for a memo that considers/proposes revising the current "authentication files" (likely the RBRA auth credential key files for service account roles like retriever/director/governor) to use FORMAL Google identities instead. The concept is moving from raw SA key files toward formal Google identity-based authentication for our service account roles.

Look at candidate files including memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md, and grep across all memos for terms like "RBRA", "authentication file", "auth file", "service account key", "formal identity", "Google identity", "workload identity", "impersonation", "ADC", "application default credentials".

Report which memo(s) match this description, with the full filename path and a 2-3 sentence summary of what each matching memo proposes. Do not modify anything.

content.text:
Based on my thorough search of the Memos directory, I found the primary memo that directly addresses your request:

## Matching Memo

**File:** `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`

**Summary:** This memo proposes moving Recipe Bottle's authentication from static RBRA service account key files (the "RBRA pattern" for Governor, Director, and Retriever roles) to a formal Google identity model using REST-only OAuth + service account impersonation. The approach eliminates key distribution logistics and improves revocation hygiene by shifting from long-lived JSON key files to user-initiated browser OAuth authentication, with temporary access tokens minted via Google's `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` API for impersonating target service accounts.

---

## Secondary References (Context Mentions)

Several other memos reference RBRA authentication files in passing but don't propose revisions:

- **memo-20260216-regime-voicing-audit.md** — Documents the RBRA regime structure and lists RBRA file paths as configuration variables
- **memo-20260209-regime-inventory.md** — Inventory of all Recipe Bottle regimes, including RBRA as a "Credential Format" regime but not proposing changes
- **memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md** — Brief mentions of credential handling in passing

The **google-native-human-auth memo is the definitive source** for your request—it's the only memo that systematically evaluates moving away from RBRA raw key files toward formal Google identities, with detailed mechanism design, threat modeling, and coexistence strategy.

Based on my thorough search of the Memos directory, I found the primary memo that directly addresses your request:

## Matching Memo

**File:** `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`

**Summary:** This memo proposes moving Recipe Bottle's authentication from static RBRA service account key files (the "RBRA pattern" for Governor, Director, and Retriever roles) to a formal Google identity model using REST-only OAuth + service account impersonation. The approach eliminates key distribution logistics and improves revocation hygiene by shifting from long-lived JSON key files to user-initiated browser OAuth authentication, with temporary access tokens minted via Google's `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` API for impersonating target service accounts.

---

## Secondary References (Context Mentions)

Several other memos reference RBRA authentication files in passing but don't propose revisions:

- **memo-20260216-regime-voicing-audit.md** — Documents the RBRA regime structure and lists RBRA file paths as configuration variables
- **memo-20260209-regime-inventory.md** — Inventory of all Recipe Bottle regimes, including RBRA as a "Credential Format" regime but not proposing changes
- **memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md** — Brief mentions of credential handling in passing

The **google-native-human-auth memo is the definitive source** for your request—it's the only memo that systematically evaluates moving away from RBRA raw key files toward formal Google identities, with detailed mechanism design, threat modeling, and coexistence strategy.

Found it: **`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`**

It proposes moving Recipe Bottle off the static **RBRA service-account key files** (the Governor / Director / Retriever auth-file pattern) toward a formal Google identity model — **REST-only OAuth + service-account impersonation**. Instead of long-lived JSON key files, the operator authenticates via browser OAuth and short-lived access tokens are minted through `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` to impersonate the target SA. The stated wins are eliminating key-distribution logistics and improving revocation hygiene.

This dovetails directly with ₢BSAAF's **key-creation block** finding (`iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` enforced by default would refuse the very SA-key creation the RBRA pattern depends on) — the memo's impersonation approach is one way out of that wall.

Want me to read it in full so we can fold its direction into the ₢BSAAF docket, or were you just locating it for reference?

message.content: read it in full in preparation for folding direction into BSAAF, but lets discuss a little first.

content.content:
1	# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-27
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
8	key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
9	roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
10	For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:
11	
12	1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
13	   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
14	   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
15	   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.
16	
17	2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
18	   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
19	   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
20	   person's access" button.
21	
22	The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
23	why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"
24	
25	## Approaches Evaluated
26	
27	### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)
28	
29	Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
30	regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
31	directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.
32	
33	- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
34	  nothing Google-specific installed.
35	- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.
36	
37	### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation
38	
39	User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
40	`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
41	`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
42	`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.
43	
44	- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
45	  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
46	- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
47	  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
48	  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
49	  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.
50	
51	Verdict: dismissed.
52	
53	### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)
54	
55	Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
56	"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
57	once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
58	Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
59	refresh token, then call
60	
61	```
62	POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
63	Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
64	```
65	
66	to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
67	`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.
68	
69	- **Pros:**
70	  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
71	  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
72	    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
73	  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
74	    binding (server-side state).
75	  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
76	    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
77	  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
78	    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
79	    model.
80	- **Cons:**
81	  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
82	    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
83	  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
84	    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
85	  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
86	    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.
87	
88	## Mechanism Detail
89	
90	### Authentication flow
91	
92	1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
93	   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
94	   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
95	   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
96	   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
97	   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
98	   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
99	   cached locally.
100	2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
101	   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
102	   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
103	   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
104	   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
105	3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
106	   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
107	   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
108	   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
109	   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
110	   the API call.
111	
112	### Storage shape
113	
114	Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:
115	
116	| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
117	|---|---|---|
118	| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
119	| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |
120	
121	Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):
122	
123	- User access token (~1 hour)
124	- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)
125	
126	Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
127	permissions, gitignored.
128	
129	### IAM binding model
130	
131	Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
132	act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
133	IAM policy on the SA itself:
134	
135	```
136	Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
137	  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
138	  Member:  user:alice@example.com
139	```
140	
141	Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
142	populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:
143	
144	```
145	                  Depot D1              Depot D2
146	alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
147	bob@...       Director              Retriever
148	carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
149	```
150	
151	Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
152	from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
153	policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
154	policies on the SAs are the registry.
155	
156	### Administrative home
157	
158	Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
159	Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
160	within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
161	rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
162	of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.
163	
164	## Coexistence with RBRA
165	
166	Additive in the near term, not a replacement:
167	
168	- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
169	  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
170	- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
171	- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
172	  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
173	  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
174	- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.
175	
176	Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
177	is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
178	this work.
179	
180	## Threat Model Summary
181	
182	| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
183	|---|---|---|
184	| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
185	| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
186	| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
187	| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
188	| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |
189	
190	The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
191	RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
192	admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.
193	
194	## Open Questions
195	
196	- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
197	  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
198	  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
199	  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
200	- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
201	  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
202	  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
203	  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
204	  screen and ToS.
205	- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
206	  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
207	  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
208	- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
209	  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
210	- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
211	  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
212	  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
213	  loads.
214	
215	## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items
216	
217	No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
218	to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
219	credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
220	mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
221	credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
222	about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
223	browser-minted tokens.
224	
225	## Trigger to Revisit
226	
227	Either:
228	
229	- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
230	- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
231	  than being theoretically frictionful).
232	
233	## Notes on Conversation Origin
234	
235	Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
236	owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
237	"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
238	"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
239	`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
240	live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
241	bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
242	Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
243	auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
244	extension on top, not a new dependency layer.
245

file.content:
# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration

Date: 2026-04-27

## Problem

Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:

1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.

2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
   person's access" button.

The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"

## Approaches Evaluated

### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)

Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.

- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
  nothing Google-specific installed.
- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.

### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation

User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.

- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.

Verdict: dismissed.

### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)

Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
refresh token, then call

```
POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
```

to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.

- **Pros:**
  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
    binding (server-side state).
  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
    model.
- **Cons:**
  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.

## Mechanism Detail

### Authentication flow

1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
   cached locally.
2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
   the API call.

### Storage shape

Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:

| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |

Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):

- User access token (~1 hour)
- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)

Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
permissions, gitignored.

### IAM binding model

Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
IAM policy on the SA itself:

```
Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
  Member:  user:alice@example.com
```

Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:

```
                  Depot D1              Depot D2
alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
bob@...       Director              Retriever
carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
```

Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
policies on the SAs are the registry.

### Administrative home

Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.

## Coexistence with RBRA

Additive in the near term, not a replacement:

- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.

Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
this work.

## Threat Model Summary

| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
|---|---|---|
| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |

The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.

## Open Questions

- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
  screen and ToS.
- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
  loads.

## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items

No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
browser-minted tokens.

## Trigger to Revisit

Either:

- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
  than being theoretically frictionful).

## Notes on Conversation Origin

Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
extension on top, not a new dependency layer.

lastPrompt: read it in full in preparation for folding direction into BSAAF, but lets discuss a little first.

content.text:
Read in full. Here's where I see the two pieces meeting — and one tension worth talking through before we fold.

**The memo's framing (2026-04-27):** It treats the keyfile→impersonation move as an *adoption/convenience* play. The two frictions it names are distribution logistics and revocation hygiene, and its "Trigger to Revisit" is explicitly demand-driven: *first external-user request*, or *first time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption*. Approach C (REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation) is recommended, B (gcloud SDK) dismissed for dependency surface, A (status quo) kept as coexisting.

**What ₢BSAAF changes:** The org-policy finding is a *third trigger the memo never anticipated*. On an org-affiliated depot, `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` is enforced **by default** — so the RBRA keyfile pattern isn't "theoretically frictionful," it's **structurally unavailable** unless the operator deliberately weakens org policy. That flips the memo's posture: impersonation stops being a future nice-to-have and becomes *the org-native path*, while keyfile mode becomes the thing that requires a policy override to keep working.

So the discussion points I'd want your read on:

1. **Is impersonation now load-bearing for the org mode, or still optional?** The memo's coexistence section says keyfile stays for headless paths (Cloud Build, fixtures, Ifrit). Those run *inside* the depot/org too — does the key-creation block hit them, or do they have a different escape (workload identity, build-time SA)? That determines whether impersonation is "the human path" or "the only path."

2. **The memo predates the reauth-cadence finding entirely.** Note its mechanism leans *harder* on OAuth refresh tokens (Approach C is literally "replicate the Payor OAuth pattern"). But ₢BSAAF just proved the org's session policy *refuses* aged refresh tokens (`invalid_rapt` at ~22h). So the very primitive the memo calls "already proven" is the one the org now constrains. That's not a contradiction — but it means the impersonation flow inherits the same reauth cadence problem, and the memo's "refresh token cached locally, mint on demand" story needs a caveat.

3. **Scope boundary for the fold.** ₢BSAAF is explicitly *design-conversation, no-code*. The memo is a rich design exploration with open questions. Folding it shouldn't pull all that detail into the docket — more likely it becomes a **named pointer + the recast trigger** ("org-policy makes this the org-native path, not just an adoption improvement"), leaving the open questions in the memo where they live.

Where do you want to push first — the headless-path question (#1), or the refresh-token/reauth collision (#2)?

message.content: Crumbs, my head is too full at the moment to work through any of this, I do want the whole picture retained in the docket, of all the issues we need to work through and the considerations.  Are you inspired yet to reslate?

content.text: Yes — inspired. The whole picture retained, the memo wired in, the three tensions captured as work-to-do rather than resolved. Let me write the reslate.

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate BSAAF

## Character

Interactive design conversation requiring operator judgment — do NOT
implement. A strategic reorientation that should reshape this heat's tactical
paces before they are cut; its product is an agreed direction, not code.

## Trigger

Surfaced live while first-running the ₣BM skirmish suite. The project's active
Payor moved from a **consumer** Google account (`bhyslop@gmail.com`, no org) to
an **org-affiliated** Workspace identity (`bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`, org
`247899326218`). The operator's read: this is a major use-style change, not a
one-off config tweak, and the project must be reoriented to treat
org-affiliated accounts as a first-class mode.

## What we proved this session (the durable reminder)

Org-affiliated identities inherit secure-by-default posture the prior consumer
account never had — two distinct behaviors, two distinct layers:

- **Reauth cadence.** The org's session/reauth policy refuses the payor OAuth
  refresh token after a short window (a ~22h-old token returned `invalid_rapt`;
  `rbw-gPI` re-auth cleared it). `rbw-gPR` is stale here — its text assumes the
  consumer "6-month idle expiry" model, which no longer describes reality.
- **Key-creation block.** `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` is `enforced`
  by default on the standing depot, so governor/retriever/director SA-key
  creation will be refused — the wall the mantle would hit next.
- The payor **lacks** `orgpolicy.policy.set` but **holds**
  `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (so it could self-grant
  policyAdmin). The standing depot is ACTIVE under the org; no levy needed.

## The memo this fold pulls in

`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` — a 2026-04-27 design
exploration proposing the project move off static RBRA service-account keyfiles
(Governor / Director / Retriever) toward formal Google identities via
**REST-only browser OAuth + SA impersonation** (Approach C; gcloud-SDK Approach
B dismissed for dependency surface; keyfile Approach A retained as coexisting).
Mechanism: user authenticates once in a browser, refresh token cached locally;
per operation, mint a user access token then call `generateAccessToken` on
`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` to obtain a short-lived SA access token. The
SA principal is preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role taxonomy,
audit identity all unchanged. Distribution dissolves (access becomes an IAM
binding); revocation becomes a single binding deletion; audit logs gain
`delegationInfo`. Read it in full before reshaping downstream paces — its
Mechanism Detail, Storage shape, IAM binding model, Coexistence, Threat Model,
and Open Questions sections are the design substrate and should NOT be
re-paraphrased here.

## How the org finding reframes the memo (the key turn)

The memo framed keyfile→impersonation as an **adoption/convenience** play — its
two named frictions are distribution logistics and revocation hygiene, and its
"Trigger to Revisit" is demand-driven (first external-user request, or first
time keyfile distribution *materially* blocks adoption). The org-policy finding
is a **third trigger the memo never anticipated**: on an org-affiliated depot,
`iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` is enforced *by default*, so the RBRA
keyfile pattern is not "theoretically frictionful" — it is **structurally
unavailable** unless the operator deliberately weakens org policy. That flips
the posture: impersonation moves from future nice-to-have toward the
**org-native path**, while keyfile mode becomes the thing requiring a policy
override to keep alive.

## Issues to work through (none resolved — captured for a fresh head)

1. **Is impersonation load-bearing for the org mode, or still optional?** The
   memo's coexistence section keeps keyfiles for headless paths (Cloud Build,
   automated fixtures, Ifrit-style scenarios). Those also run inside the
   depot/org. Open: does the key-creation block hit them too, or do they have a
   different escape (workload identity, build-time SA)? The answer decides
   whether impersonation is "the human path" or "the only path."

2. **The memo predates the reauth-cadence finding and collides with it.**
   Approach C is literally "replicate the Payor OAuth pattern" and leans *harder*
   on OAuth refresh tokens — yet this session proved the org's session policy
   *refuses* aged refresh tokens (`invalid_rapt` at ~22h). The very primitive the
   memo calls "already proven" is the one the org now constrains. The
   impersonation flow inherits the same reauth cadence; the memo's "refresh token
   cached locally, mint on demand" story needs that caveat.

3. **`rbw-gPR` (PayorRefresh) is stale.** Its "6-month idle expiry" text
   describes the consumer model, not the org's short reauth window. Whatever
   direction we land, this surface needs rewriting — and it interacts with #2,
   since impersonation onboarding would carry the same reauth assumption forward
   if copied verbatim.

4. **Org-policy override ownership.** The payor holds
   `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (could self-grant policyAdmin)
   but lacks `orgpolicy.policy.set`. If keyfile mode is to survive on org
   depots, *someone* must own relaxing `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` — and
   where that override is surfaced, owned, and documented (establish / levy /
   onboarding) is part of the org-mode design, not a side note.

5. **Memo's own Open Questions remain open and feed downstream paces:** storage
   location (regime-tree vs `$HOME`), OAuth client scope (project-wide vs
   per-Manor), onboarding handbook track shape (parallel RBHO* vs unified
   branching), probe coexistence / mode dispatch (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV), and mode
   field naming/placement. These live in the memo; this pace decides which
   become tactical paces in this heat.

## Goal

Decide, honestly and with the operator, how org-affiliated accounts become a
supported use style: where reauth cadence and the key-creation override are
surfaced, owned, and documented across establish / levy / onboarding; whether
the keyfile→impersonation move (per the 2026-04-27 memo) is now load-bearing for
the org mode rather than a future convenience; and whether the tactical
org-policy paces already slated in this heat are the right shape or need
recasting in light of the above. Output: an agreed design direction written back
into the paddock, with the downstream paces reshaped to match.

## Constraint

Produces understanding and a reshaped plan, not code. Implementation belongs to
the downstream paces, governed by this pace's conclusions.

content.content: File created successfully at: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/.claude/jjm/officia/260522-1005/gazette_in.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

+ B dismissed for dependency surface; keyfile Approach A retained as coexisting).

+    Approach C is literally "replicate the Payor OAuth pattern" and leans *harder*
+    on OAuth refresh tokens — yet this session proved the org's session policy
+    *refuses* aged refresh tokens (`invalid_rapt` at ~22h). The very primitive the
+    memo calls "already proven" is the one the org now constrains. The
+    impersonation flow inherits the same reauth cadence; the memo's "refresh token
+    cached locally, mint on demand" story needs that caveat.
+ 
+ 3. **`rbw-gPR` (PayorRefresh) is stale.** Its "6-month idle expiry" text
+    describes the consumer model, not the org's short reauth window. Whatever
+    direction we land, this surface needs rewriting — and it interacts with #2,
+    since impersonation onboarding would carry the same reauth assumption forward
+    if copied verbatim.
+ 
+ 4. **Org-policy override ownership.** The payor holds
+    `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (could self-grant policyAdmin)
+    but lacks `orgpolicy.policy.set`. If keyfile mode is to survive on org
+    depots, *someone* must own relaxing `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` — and
+    where that override is surfaced, owned, and documented (establish / levy /
+    onboarding) is part of the org-mode design, not a side note.
+ 
+ 5. **Memo's own Open Questions remain open and feed downstream paces:** storage
+    location (regime-tree vs `$HOME`), OAuth client scope (project-wide vs
+    per-Manor), onboarding handbook track shape (parallel RBHO* vs unified
+    branching), probe coexistence / mode dispatch (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV), and mode
+    field naming/placement. These live in the memo; this pace decides which
+    become tactical paces in this heat.
+ 
- surfaced, owned, and documented across establish / levy / onboarding — and
- whether the tactical org-policy paces already slated in this heat are the right
- shape or need recasting in light of the above. Output: an agreed design
- direction written back into the paddock, with the downstream paces reshaped to
- match.

toolUseResult.text:
Revised 1 pace(s)

--- ₢BSAAF reslate diff ---
+ ## The memo this fold pulls in
+ 
+ `Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` — a 2026-04-27 design
+ exploration proposing the project move off static RBRA service-account keyfiles
+ (Governor / Director / Retriever) toward formal Google identities via
+ **REST-only browser OAuth + SA impersonation** (Approach C; gcloud-SDK Approach
+ B dismissed for dependency surface; keyfile Approach A retained as coexisting).
+ Mechanism: user authenticates once in a browser, refresh token cached locally;
+ per operation, mint a user access token then call `generateAccessToken` on
+ `iamcredentials.googleapis.com` to obtain a short-lived SA access token. The
+ SA principal is preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role taxonomy,
+ audit identity all unchanged. Distribution dissolves (access becomes an IAM
+ binding); revocation becomes a single binding deletion; audit logs gain
+ `delegationInfo`. Read it in full before reshaping downstream paces — its
+ Mechanism Detail, Storage shape, IAM binding model, Coexistence, Threat Model,
+ and Open Questions sections are the design substrate and should NOT be
+ re-paraphrased here.
+ 
+ ## How the org finding reframes the memo (the key turn)
+ 
+ The memo framed keyfile→impersonation as an **adoption/convenience** play — its
+ two named frictions are distribution logistics and revocation hygiene, and its
+ "Trigger to Revisit" is demand-driven (first external-user request, or first
+ time keyfile distribution *materially* blocks adoption). The org-policy finding
+ is a **third trigger the memo never anticipated**: on an org-affiliated depot,
+ `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` is enforced *by default*, so the RBRA
+ keyfile pattern is not "theoretically frictionful" — it is **structurally
+ unavailable** unless the operator deliberately weakens org policy. That flips
+ the posture: impersonation moves from future nice-to-have toward the
+ **org-native path**, while keyfile mode becomes the thing requiring a policy
+ override to keep alive.
+ 
+ ## Issues to work through (none resolved — captured for a fresh head)
+ 
+ 1. **Is impersonation load-bearing for the org mode, or still optional?** The
+    memo's coexistence section keeps keyfiles for headless paths (Cloud Build,
+    automated fixtures, Ifrit-style scenarios). Those also run inside the
+    depot/org. Open: does the key-creation block hit them too, or do they have a
+    different escape (workload identity, build-time SA)? The answer decides
+    whether impersonation is "the human path" or "the only path."
+ 
+ 2. **The memo predates the reauth-cadence finding and collides with it.**
+    Approach C is literally "replicate the Payor OAuth pattern" and leans *harder*
+    on OAuth refresh tokens — yet this session proved the org's session policy
+    *refuses* aged refresh tokens (`invalid_rapt` at ~22h). The very primitive the
+    memo calls "already proven" is the one the org now constrains. The
+    impersonation flow inherits the same reauth cadence; the memo's "refresh token
+    cached locally, mint on demand" story needs that caveat.
+ 
+ 3. **`rbw-gPR` (PayorRefresh) is stale.** Its "6-month idle expiry" text
+    describes the consumer model, not the org's short reauth window. Whatever
+    direction we land, this surface needs rewriting — and it interacts with #2,
+    since impersonation onboarding would carry the same reauth assumption forward
+    if copied verbatim.
+ 
+ 4. **Org-policy override ownership.** The payor holds
+    `resourcemanager.organizations.setIamPolicy` (could self-grant policyAdmin)
+    but lacks `orgpolicy.policy.set`. If keyfile mode is to survive on org
+    depots, *someone* must own relaxing `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` — and
+    where that override is surfaced, owned, and documented (establish / levy /
+    onboarding) is part of the org-mode design, not a side note.
+ 
+ 5. **Memo's own Open Questions remain open and feed downstream paces:** storage
+    location (regime-tree vs `$HOME`), OAuth client scope (project-wide vs
+    per-Manor), onboarding handbook track shape (parallel RBHO* vs unified
+    branching), probe coexistence / mode dispatch (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV), and mode
+    field naming/placement. These live in the memo; this pace decides which
+    become tactical paces in this heat.
+ 
- surfaced, owned, and documented across establish / levy / onboarding — and
- whether the tactical org-policy paces already slated in this heat are the right
- shape or need recasting in light of the above. Output: an agreed design
- direction written back into the paddock, with the downstream paces reshaped to

lastPrompt: Crumbs, my head is too full at the moment to work through any of this, I do want the whole picture retained in the docket, of all the issues we need to work through and the considerations.  Are you in…

content.text:
Reslated **₢BSAAF**. The docket now retains the whole picture for when you have headspace:

- **The memo, named and pointed-to** (`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`) — summarized, with an explicit instruction *not* to re-paraphrase its detail since it lives in the memo.
- **The key turn** — how the org-policy finding reframes that memo from convenience-play to org-native-path.
- **Five issues to work through**, none resolved: impersonation load-bearing-or-optional (headless paths), the refresh-token/reauth collision, stale `rbw-gPR`, org-policy override ownership, and the memo's own open questions feeding downstream paces.
- Goal updated to fold in the keyfile→impersonation question; Trigger / What-we-proved / Constraint preserved unchanged.

The pace stays *design-conversation, no-code*. Nothing committed beyond the redocket's own internal commit. When your head clears, mounting ₢BSAAF will surface all of this fresh.

content: We're reshaping heat ₣BS to treat org-affiliated Google accounts as first-class. Pace ₢BSAAF now holds the full design picture, including the auth-impersonation memo and five open issues. Next: mount ₢BSAAF to work through those issues when ready. (disable recaps in /config)
